### Original:
both historical accuracy and a display of Oriental luxury, but the drama may easily be performed with simple means at a small cost without losing its dramatic effect. Some of the changes, however, should be very rapid. The interludes can be replaced by lantern slide pictures, or may be omitted. If the interludes are retained there need not be any intermission in the whole drama. The music for the Buddha's Hymn of Victory, pages 5 and 39 (see _The Open Court_, XIX, 49); the dirge on page 19, (_Open Court_, XIX, 567); Yasodhara's Song, page 37 (_Open Court_, XVIII, 625); and the Doxology, page 63 and at the end (_Open Court_, XVIII, 627), may be found in a collection entitled _Buddhist Hymns_ (Chicago, Open Court Publishing Co., 1911). ### Plain English:
You can put on this play with fancy sets and decorations if you want to show real history and Asian luxury. But you can also do it with simple stuff and a small budget and it will still be dramatic. Some parts just need to happen really fast. The parts between scenes can be replaced with pictures shown on a screen, or you can skip them completely. If you keep those parts, you don't need to stop the play at all. The music for different songs in the play can be found in a book called _Buddhist Hymns_ (Chicago, Open Court Publishing Co., 1911). This play is copyrighted by The Open Court Publishing Co. in 1913.

CAST OF CHARACTERS
_Say all vowels like they sound in Italian._

Siddháttha Gótama, Prince of the Sakyas, who later becomes the Buddha _B_
Suddhodana, King of the Sakyas, father of Siddháttha _S_
Pajapati, Queen of the Sakyas, aunt and stepmother of Siddháttha _P_
Princess Yasodhara, Siddháttha's wife _Y_
Rahula, Yasodhara's son _R_
Devadatta, brother of Yasodhara _Dd_
Kala Udayin, a

### Original:
Illustrated Philadelphia George W. Jacobs & Co. 1900 TO MY MOTHER SARAH MAXWELL LUMMIS, THE DAUGHTER OF A REVOLUTIONARY OFFICER, THIS WORK IS RESPECTFULLY AND AFFECTIONATELY INSCRIBED INTRODUCTION. When Mrs. Ellet compiled her history of "The Women of the Revolution," she could not have foreseen the deep interest in Colonial and Revolutionary history, that was destined to mark the last decade of the Nineteenth Century, nor could she have realized that the various patriotic societies that were to be organized among women, would lead to as great an interest in the lives of the mothers as in those of the fathers of the Republic. Yet the writer of these sketches of noted women has prepared for just such a phase of American life, which makes her work now appear a prophecy of the future as well as a summary of past events. "The Women of the Revolution" having been published in the middle of the century, the material for these biographical sketches was collected while some men and women were still living who could recall the faces and figures of the statesmen and soldiers of the Revolutionary struggle. When the sketch of Mrs. Philip Schuyler went to press, the daughter

### Plain English:
Philadelphia, Published by George W. Jacobs & Co., 1900. This book is dedicated to my mother, Sarah Maxwell Lummis. She was the daughter of a man who fought in the American Revolution. I dedicate this work to her with respect and love.

INTRODUCTION

When Mrs. Ellet wrote her history called "The Women of the Revolution," she did not know how much people would care about Colonial and Revolutionary history. This interest became very strong in the last ten years of the 1800s. She also could not have guessed that women's groups would form across the country. These groups would become just as interested in the mothers of America as they were in the fathers of America. But the person who wrote these stories about famous women was ready for exactly this moment in American life. Her work now looks like she predicted the future. It also sums up what happened in the past. "The Women of the Revolution" came out in the middle of the century. So the stories in this book were collected while some people who lived during the Revolution were still alive. They could remember what the leaders and soldiers of the Revolution actually looked like. When the story about Mrs. Philip Schuyler was ready to be printed, her daughter...

### Plain English:
You can perform this play with fancy sets and Oriental decorations, but you don't have to spend a lot of money. You can do it with simple materials and it will still work as a drama. Some scenes need to change very quickly. You can use slide pictures between scenes instead of having live interludes, or you can skip them completely. If you keep the interludes, you don't need any breaks in the whole play. The music for the Buddha's Hymn of Victory (pages 5 and 39), the sad song on page 19, Yasodhara's Song on page 37, and the Doxology on pages 63 and at the end can be found in a book called Buddhist Hymns (Chicago, Open Court Publishing Co., 1911). ### Original:
SERIES Stories of Frank and Dick Merriwell =PRICE, FIFTEEN CENTS= _=Fascinating Stories of Athletics=_ A half million enthusiastic followers of the Merriwell brothers will attest the unfailing interest and wholesomeness of these adventures of two lads of high ideals, who play fair with themselves, as well as with the rest of the world. These stories are rich in fun and thrills in all branches of sports and athletics. They are extremely high in moral tone, and cannot fail to be of immense benefit to every boy who reads them. They have the splendid quality of firing a boy’s ambition to become a good athlete, in order that he may develop into a strong, vigorous right-thinking man. _ALL TITLES ALWAYS IN PRINT_ 1—Frank Merriwell’s School Days By Burt L. Standish 2—Frank Merriwell’s Chums By Burt L. Standish 3—Frank Merriwell’s Foes By Burt L. Standish 4—Frank Merriwell’s Trip West By Burt L. Standish 5—Frank Merriwell Down South By Burt L. Standish 6—Frank Merriwell’s Bravery By Burt L. Standish 7—Frank Merriwell’s Hunting Tour By Burt L. Standish 8—Frank Merriwell in Europe By Burt L. Standish 9—Frank Merriwell at Yale By Burt L. Standish 10—Frank Merriwell’s Sports Afield By Burt L. Standish 11—Frank Merriwell’s

### Plain English:
# Frank and Dick Merriwell Stories

**Price: 15 cents**

**Great Sports Stories**

Half a million fans love the Merriwell brothers. These books are exciting and clean. They're about two guys who have good values. They play sports fairly and treat people right. The stories are full of fun and thrills about all kinds of sports. They have strong messages about doing the right thing. Reading them will help any boy become a better person. These books make boys want to be good athletes. That way they can grow up to be strong, smart, and good men.

**All books are always available**

1—Frank Merriwell's School Days by Burt L. Standish

2—Frank Merriwell's Chums by Burt L. Standish

3—Frank Merriwell's Foes by Burt L. Standish

4—Frank Merriwell's Trip West by Burt L. Standish

10—Frank Merriwell's Sports Afield by Burt L. Standish

### Original:
THE MACMILLAN COMPANY OF CANADA LIMITED _Toronto_ ST MARTIN'S PRESS INC _New York_ PRINTED IN GREAT BRITAIN AUTHOR'S NOTE About thirty years since, several of "The Bab Ballads" (most of which had appeared, from time to time, in the pages of _Fun_) were collected by me, and published by Messrs. George Routledge and Sons. This volume passed through several editions, and, in due course, was followed by a second series under the title of "More Bab Ballads," which achieved a popularity equal to that of its predecessor. Subsequently, excerpts were made from these two volumes, and, under the title of "Fifty Bab Ballads," had a very considerable sale; but I soon discovered that in making the selection for this volume I had discarded certain Ballads that were greater favourites with my readers than with me. Nevertheless this issue was followed by many editions, English and American, of "Bab Ballads," "More Bab Ballads," and "Fifty Bab Ballads," to the no little bewilderment of such of the public as had been good enough to concern themselves with my verses. So it became desirable (for our own private ends) that this confusion should be definitely cleared up; and thus it came to pass

### Plain English:
THE MACMILLAN COMPANY OF CANADA LIMITED _Toronto_ ST MARTIN'S PRESS INC _New York_ PRINTED IN GREAT BRITAIN AUTHOR'S NOTE About thirty years ago, I collected several of "The Bab Ballads." Most of these had been printed in a magazine called _Fun_. I published them with George Routledge and Sons. This book came out in several editions. Later, I made a second book called "More Bab Ballads." It was just as popular as the first one. Then I took pieces from both books and made a new book called "Fifty Bab Ballads." It sold really well. But I found out something. The ballads I left out were actually more popular with readers than the ones I chose. Still, after that book came out, there were many different editions. Some came out in England and some in America. There were copies of "Bab Ballads," "More Bab Ballads," and "Fifty Bab Ballads." This confused a lot of people who liked my work. So I decided to fix this mess and make things clear. That's why I'm doing this now.
